title.english: Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream: Language and Place
title.teReoMāori: Ngā Kaitiaki o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Te Reo me Te Wāhi

subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that gives our college its name. This lesson connects to Ngāti Kahungunu traditions of environmental guardianship (kaitiakitanga) and the pūrākau (traditional stories) associated with this waterway, which was historically known for its kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) and as a source of sustenance and healing.
learningObjectives: Identify and use at least 10 Māori kupu (words) related to freshwater ecosystems and kaitiakitanga in spoken and written contexts
Recount the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate sentence structures and sequencing language in Te Reo Māori
Create a simple pepeha (introduction) that includes personal connection to the local awa (stream) using correct pronunciation and cultural protocols
Collaborate in small groups to create a waiata (song) or karakia (incantation) for the awa that incorporates newly learned vocabulary

activities.title: He Whakawhitinga Kōrero: Introducing Our Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia for the awa. Using large visual maps and aerial photographs, students will learn and practice key vocabulary (awa, wai, kaitiaki, kotukutuku, etc.) through interactive games like 'E ai ki te kupu' (According to the word) where they physically place labels on corresponding features.

activities.title: Te Hītori me te Pūrākau: The Story of Our Stream
activities.description: Watch a pre-recorded video of a local kaumātua (elder) from Ngāti Kahungunu sharing the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students work in pairs to sequence illustrated story cards and retell the main events to each other using sentence starters like 'I mua...', 'Kātahi...', 'Nā reira...' (In the past..., Then..., Therefore...).

activities.title: Taku Pepeha, Taku Awa
activities.description: Using a scaffolded template, students will craft their own pepeha that includes the line 'Ko [Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku] tōku awa' ([Mangakōtukutuku Stream] is my stream). They will practice pronunciation with a buddy, focusing on the macronised vowels and rolled 'r's specific to our local dialect.

activities.title: He Waiata mō te Awa: A Song for the Stream
activities.description: In groups (rōpū), students will compose a simple, repetitive waiata or a short karakia to express their role as kaitiaki of the awa. They must incorporate at least five of the new kupu learned. Groups will practice and perform their waiata for the class.

activities.title: Te Haerenga: Visit to the Awa
activities.description: A short walk to the banks of the stream (if accessible and safe) or a virtual tour using 360-degree images. Students will use their knowledge to identify features, recite their pepeha on the whenua (land), and sing their newly composed waiata as an offering to the awa.

resources: Video recording of Uncle Hōne Mitchell (Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua) sharing the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Te Aka Māori Dictionary online (https://maoridictionary.co.nz/) and the 'He Pātaka Kupu' book series
Large-scale laminated maps and recent aerial photographs of the local area featuring the stream
Scaffolded pepeha templates and vocabulary flashcards developed by the college's Te Reo department

tasks: Completion of a labelled diagram of the awa ecosystem using at least 10 correct Māori kupu
Oral recording of the student's personal pepeha, assessed for pronunciation, content, and confidence
Group performance of the composed waiata/karakia, assessed for use of vocabulary, collaboration, and cultural appropriateness

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Mana Reo (Level 3-4: Use basic Māori language patterns and vocabulary to communicate about immediate and familiar environments)
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Mana Reo (Level 3-4: Recognise that Māori is the language of the tangata whenua of Aotearoa New Zealand and understand how language and culture are inseparable)
Social Sciences: Place and Environment - Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways (Level 3)
Key Competencies: Participating and Contributing - Students will contribute to a group task and explore their role within a local cultural context
